title: Pompilia
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am just seventeen years and five months old,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, if I lived one day more, three full weeks;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis writ so in the churchן¿½s register,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lorenzo in Lucina, all my names
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At length, so many names for one poor child,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Francesca Camilla Vittoria Angela
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pompilia Comparini, laughable!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Also ן¿½tis writ that I was married there
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Four years ago; and they will add, I hope,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When they insert my death, a word or two,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Omitting all about the mode of death,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This, in its place, this which one cares to know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That I had been a mother of a son
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Exactly two weeks. It will be through grace
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½ the Curate, not through any claim I have;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because the boy was born at, so baptised
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Close to, the Villa, in the proper church:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A pretty church, I say no word against,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet stranger-like, while this Lorenzo seems
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My own particular place, I always say.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I used to wonder, when I stood scarce high
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the bed here, what the marble lion meant,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With half his body rushing from the wall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Eating the figure of a prostrate man
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (To the right, it is, of entry by the door)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ An ominous sign to one baptised like me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Married, and to be buried there, I hope.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they should add, to have my life complete,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He is a boy and Gaetan by name
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gaetano, for a reason, if the friar
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Don Celestine will ask this grace for me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Curate Ottoboni: he it was
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Baptised me: he remembers my whole life
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As I do his grey hair.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All these few things
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I know are true, will you remember them?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because time flies. The surgeon cared for me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To count my wounds, twenty-two dagger-wounds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Five deadly, but I do not suffer much
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or too much pain, and am to die to-night.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh how good God is that my babe was born,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Better than born, baptised and hid away
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before this happened, safe from being hurt!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That had been sin God could not well forgive:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was too young to smile and save himself.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When they took, two days after he was born,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My babe away from me to be baptised
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hidden awhile, for fear his foe should find,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The country-woman, used to nursing babes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said ן¿½Why take on so? where is the great loss?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½These next three weeks he will but sleep and feed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Only begin to smile at the monthן¿½s end;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He would not know you, if you kept him here,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sooner than that; so, spend three merry weeks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Snug in the Villa, getting strong and stout,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And then I bring him back to be your own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And both of you may steal to we know where!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The month there wants of it two weeks this day!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still, I half fancied when I heard the knock
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At the Villa in the dusk, it might prove she
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Come to say ן¿½Since he smiles before the time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why should I cheat you out of one good hour?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Back I have brought him; speak to him and judge!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now I shall never see him; what is worse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he grows up and gets to be my age,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He will seem hardly more than a great boy;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And if he asks ן¿½What was my mother like?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ People may answer ן¿½Like girls of seventeenן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And how can he but think of this and that,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lucias, Marias, Sofias, who titter or blush
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When he regards them as such boys may do?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Therefore I wish some one will please to say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I looked already old though I was young;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Do I not . . . say, if you are by to speak . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look nearer twenty? No more like, at least,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Girls who look arch or redden when boys laugh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than the poor Virgin that I used to know
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At our street-corner in a lonely niche,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The babe, that sat upon her knees, broke off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thin white glazed clay, you pitied her the more:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She, not the gay ones, always got my rose.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How happy those are who know how to write!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such could write what their son should read in time,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had they a whole day to live out like me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Also my name is not a common name,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Pompilia,ן¿½ and may help to keep apart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A little the thing I am from what girls are.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But then how far away, how hard to find
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will anything about me have become,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even if the boy bethink himself and ask!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No father that he ever knew at all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor ever had no, never had, I say!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That is the truth, nor any mother left,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of the little two weeks that she lived,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Fit for such memory as might assist:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As good too as no family, no name,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not even poor old Pietroן¿½s name, nor hers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poor kind unwise Violante, since it seems
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They must not be my parents any more.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That is why something put it in my head
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To call the boy ן¿½Gaetanoן¿½ no old name
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For sorrowן¿½s sake; I looked up to the sky
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And took a new saint to begin anew.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One who has only been made saint how long?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Twenty-five years: so, carefuller, perhaps,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To guard a namesake than those old saints grow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tired out by this time, see my own five saints!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On second thoughts, I hope he will regard
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The history of me as what someone dreamed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And get to disbelieve it at the last:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since to myself it dwindles fast to that,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sheer dreaming and impossibility,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just in four days too! All the seventeen years,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not once did a suspicion visit me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How very different a lot is mine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From any other womanן¿½s in the world.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The reason must be, ן¿½twas by step and step
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It got to grow so terrible and strange:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These strange woes stole on tiptoe, as it were,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into my neighbourhood and privacy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sat down where I sat, laid them where I lay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I was found familiarised with fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When friends broke in, held up a torch and cried
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why, you Pompilia in the cavern thus,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How comes that arm of yours about a wolf?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And the soft length, lies in and out your feet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And laps you round the knee, a snake it is!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so on.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, and they are right enough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the torch they hold up now: for first, observe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I never had a father, no, nor yet
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A mother: my own boy can say at least
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I had a mother whom I kept two weeks!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not I, who little used to doubt . . . I
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good Pietro, kind Violante, gave me birth?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They loved me always as I love my babe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ( Nearly so, that is quite so could not be )
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did for me all I meant to do for him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till one surprising day, three years ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They both declared, at Rome, before some judge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In some court where the people flocked to hear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That really I had never been their child,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was a mere castaway, the careless crime
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of an unknown man, the crime and care too much
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a woman known too well, little to these,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Therefore, of whom I was the flesh and blood:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What then to Pietro and Violante, both
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No more my relatives than you or you?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nothing to them! You know what they declared.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So with my husband, just such a surprise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such a mistake, in that relationship!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Everyone says that husbands love their wives,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Guard them and guide them, give them happiness;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis duty, law, pleasure, religion: well,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You see how much of this comes true in mine!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ People indeed would fain have somehow proved
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was no husband: but he did not hear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or would not wait and so has killed us all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then there is . . . only let me name one more!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is the friend, men will not ask about,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But tell untruths of, and give nicknames to,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And think my lover, most surprise of all!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Do only hear, it is the priest they mean,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Giuseppe Caponsacchi: a priest love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And love me! Well, yet people think he did.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am married, he has taken priestly vows,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They know that, and yet go on, say, the same,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yes, how he loves you!ן¿½ ן¿½That was loveן¿½ they say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When anything is answered that they ask:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or else ן¿½No wonder you love himן¿½ they say.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then they shake heads, pity much, scarcely blame
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As if we neither of us lacked excuse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And anyhow are punished to the full,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And downright love atones for everything!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nay, I heard read-out in the public court
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before the judge, in presence of my friends,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Letters ן¿½twas said the priest had sent to me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And other letters sent him by myself,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We being lovers!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Listen what this is like!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I was a mere child, my mother . . . thatן¿½s
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Violante, you must let me call her so
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor waste time, trying to unlearn the word, . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She brought a neighbourן¿½s child of my own age
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To play with me of rainy afternoons;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, since there hung a tapestry on the wall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We two agreed to find each other out
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Among the figures. ן¿½Tisbe, that is you,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With half-moon on your hair-knot, spear in hand,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Flying, but no wings, only the great scarf
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Blown to a bluish rainbow at your back:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Call off your hound and leave the stag alone!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And there are you, Pompilia, such green leaves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Flourishing out of your five finger-ends,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And all the rest of you are turned a sort of tree?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why is it you are turned a sort of tree?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know the figures never were ourselves
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though we nicknamed them so. Thus, all my life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As well what was, as what, like this, was not,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Looks old, fantastic and impossible:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I touch a fairy thing that fades and fades.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Even to my babe! I thought, when he was born,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Something began for once that would not end,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor change into a laugh at me, but stay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For evermore, eternally quite mine.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, so he is, but yet they bore him off,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The third day, lest my husband should lay traps
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And catch him, and by means of him catch me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since they have saved him so, it was well done:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet thence comes such confusion of what was
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With what will be, that late seems long ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, what years should bring round, already come,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till even he withdraws into a dream
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the rest do: I fancy him grown great,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Strong, stern, a tall young man who tutors me,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Frowns with the others ן¿½Poor imprudent child!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why did you venture out of the safe street?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why go so far from help to that lone house?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why open at the whisper and the knock?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Six days ago when it was New Yearן¿½s-day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We bent above the fire and talked of him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What he should do when he was grown and great.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Violante, Pietro, each had given the arm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I leant on, to walk by, from couch to chair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fireside, laughed, as I lay safe at last,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Pompiliaן¿½s march from bed to board is made,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Pompilia back again and with a babe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Shall one day lend his arm and help her walk!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then we all wished each other more New Years.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pietro began to scheme ן¿½Our cause is gained;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The law is stronger than a wicked man:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Let him henceforth go his way, leave us ours!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½We will avoid the city, tempt no more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The greedy ones by feasting and parade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Live at the other villa, we know where,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Still farther off, and we can watch the babe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Grow fast in the good air; and wood is cheap
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And wine sincere outside the city gate.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I still have two or three old friends will grope
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Their way along the mere half-mile of road,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With staff and lantern on a moonless night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When one needs talk: theyן¿½ll find me, never fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And Iן¿½ll find them a flask of the old sort yet!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Violante said ן¿½You chatter like a crow:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Pompilia tires oן¿½ the tattle, and shall to-bed:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Do not too much the first day, somewhat more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To-morrow, and, the next, begin the cape
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And hood and coat! I have spun wool enough.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh what a happy friendly eve was that!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, next day, about noon, out Pietro went
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was so happy and would talk so much,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Until Violante pushed and laughed him forth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sight-seeing in the cold, ן¿½So much to see
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ the churches! Swathe your throat three times!ן¿½ she cried,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, above all, beware the slippery ways,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And bring us all the news by supper-time!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He came back late, laid by cloak, staff and hat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Powdered so thick with snow it made us laugh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rolled a great log upon the ash oן¿½ the hearth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And bade Violante treat us to a flask,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Because he had obeyed her faithfully,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gone sight-see through the seven, and found no church
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To his mind like San Giovanni ן¿½Thereן¿½s the fold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And all the sheep together, big as cats!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And such a shepherd, half the size of life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Starts up and hears the angelן¿½ when, at the door,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A tap: we started up: you know the rest.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pietro at least had done no harm, I know;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor even Violante, so much harm as makes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such revenge lawful. Certainly she erred
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did wrong, how shall I dare say otherwise?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In telling that first falsehood, buying me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From my poor faulty mother at a price,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To pass off upon Pietro as his child:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If one should take my babe, give him a name,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Say he was not Gaetano and my own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But that some other woman made his mouth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And hands and feet, how very false were that!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No good could come of that; and all harm did.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet if a stranger were to represent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Needs must you either give your babe to me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And let me call him mine for ever more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Or let your husband get himן¿½ ah, my God,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That were a trial I refuse to face!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, just so here: it proved wrong but seemed right
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To poor Violante for there lay, she said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My poor real dying mother in her rags,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who put me from her with the life and all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Poverty, pain, shame and disease at once,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To die the easier by what price I fetched
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Also (I hope) because I should be spared
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sorrow and sin, why may not that have helped?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My father, he was no one, any one,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The worse, the likelier, call him, he who came,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was wicked for his pleasure, went his way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And left no trace to track by; there remained
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nothing but me, the unnecessary life,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To catch up or let fall, and yet a thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She could make happy, be made happy with,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This poor Violante, who would frown thereat?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well, God, you see! God plants us where we grow.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It is not that, because a bud is born
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At a wild briarן¿½s end, full iן¿½ the wild beastן¿½s way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We ought to pluck and put it out of reach
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the oak-tree top, say, ן¿½There the bud belongs!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She thought, moreover, real lies were lies told
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For harmן¿½s sake; whereas this had good at heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good for my mother, good for me, and good
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Pietro who was meant to love a babe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And needed one to make his life of use,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Receive his house and land when he should die.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wrong, wrong and always wrong! how plainly wrong!
